title: Deploying to Vercel
intro: It's deployment day. You connect your repo to Vercel, hit deploy, and... the build fails. Welcome to the real world of deployment debugging.

teach.title: Deploying a Next.js App
teach.body: Vercel is built by the creators of Next.js, making it the smoothest deployment option. You connect your GitHub repo, Vercel detects it's a Next.js app, and builds it automatically. But 'automatic' doesn't mean 'no issues' — missing env vars, build errors, and type errors are common first-deploy problems.

teach.title: Common First-Deploy Errors
teach.code:
Error: NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL is not defined
→ You forgot to add env vars in Vercel dashboard

Type error: Property 'title' does not exist on type 'never'
→ TypeScript is stricter in production builds

Error: Module not found: Can't resolve './components/TaskList'
→ Case sensitivity: taskList.tsx vs TaskList.tsx (Linux is case-sensitive)
teach.annotation: Most first-deploy failures are environment or build configuration issues, not code bugs.

teach.body: Always run the production build locally (npm run build) before deploying. It catches most build errors that won't appear in development mode.

start.narrative: You push your task manager to GitHub and connect the repo to Vercel. The build starts and immediately fails with: 'Error: NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL is not defined.' What do you do?

choices.text: Hardcode the Supabase URL directly in the code so the build doesn't need env vars
Add the environment variables in the Vercel project settings dashboard, then redeploy
Push your .env file to the repo so Vercel can read it

hardcode.narrative: The build passes, but now your Supabase URL is visible in the public source code. Anyone can find your database endpoint. A security-conscious colleague flags the issue.
hardcode.feedback: Even URLs should be treated as configuration, not hardcoded values. Environment variables exist precisely for values that change between environments and should not be public.

add-env-vars.narrative: You add the env vars in Vercel settings. The build passes. But the app shows a blank page. The browser console reveals: 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined.' What's your next step?

choices.text: Check Vercel's deployment logs and the browser console to find the exact failing component
Run npm run build locally to reproduce the issue in a local production build

push-env.narrative: You accidentally push your .env file with your real Supabase service role key. Within minutes, a bot scraping GitHub finds it. You get an email from Supabase warning about exposed credentials. You immediately rotate keys and add .env to .gitignore.
push-env.feedback: Never push .env files to Git. Even after deletion, secrets remain in Git history. Always add .env to .gitignore before your first commit, and rotate any leaked credentials immediately.

debug-logs.narrative: The logs show a component is trying to read a property from data that hasn't loaded yet. You add a loading state check and the app works perfectly on the next deploy.
debug-logs.feedback: Good debugging process. Deployment logs and browser console are your best friends. The loading state issue is common — data fetching behaves differently in production vs development.

local-build.narrative: Running 'npm run build' locally reveals the exact same error with a full stack trace pointing to a component that doesn't handle the loading state. You fix it locally, push, and Vercel auto-deploys successfully.
local-build.feedback: Running the production build locally is the best practice. It reproduces most deployment issues with a faster feedback loop than pushing and waiting for Vercel to build.
